coredata.dc:title: Dynamically mechanochromic, fluorescence-responsive, and underwater sensing cellulose nanocrystal-based conductive elastomers

coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Hongtian
Cao, Yunfeng
Hu, Yongke
Liu, Zhulan
Li, Ren'ai
coredata.dc:description: Utilizing cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) to mimic biological skin capable of converting external stimuli into optical and electrical signals represents a significant advancement in the development of advanced photonic materials. However, traditional CNC photonic materials typically exhibit static and singular optical properties, with their structural color and mechanical performance being susceptible to water molecules, thereby limiting their practical applications. In this study, CNC-based conductive elastomers with dynamic mechanochromism, fluorescence responsiveness, and enhanced water resistance were developed by incorporating carbon quantum dots (C QDs) and hydrophobic deep eutectic solvents (HDES) into CNC photonic films via an in-situ swelling-photopolymerization method. Additionally, the abundant non-covalent and hydrophobic interactions within poly(HDES) endow the resulting poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer with excellent structural stability, ionic conductivity, and self-adhesive properties, allowing for encrypted information transmission in both air and aquatic environments. The design and application of poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomers hold tremendous potential for various applications, including anti-counterfeiting labels, human-machine interactions, and wearable devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cellulose nanocrystals
Hydrophobic deep eutectic solvents
Underwater sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer
Enhanced water resistance and mechanochromic response

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the preparation of poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer through the incorporation of carbon quantum dots (C QDs) and hydrophobic deep eutectic solvents (HDES) into CNC networks. (b) Comparison of pure CNC film and poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer: The pure CNC film easily fractures underwater, while the poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer retains its overall integrity and is less affected by water. Additionally, the incorporation of C QDs imparts fluorescence to the poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer under UV light.

(a) Optical photographs of C/Glu-CNC photonic film under natural light. (b) The color change of C/Glu-CNC photonic film under natural (top) and UV light (down) after different swelling times in DMSO and HDES prepolymer. (c) The cross-section SEM images of C/Glu-CNC photonic film and (d-e) poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer. (f–g) Elemental distributions of N and Cl on the cross-section of poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer. (h) XRD patterns of C/Glu-CNC photonic film and poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer. (i) FT-IR spectra of HDES, C/Glu-CNC photonic film, and poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer. (j–k) XPS spectra and high-resolution C 1s of C/Glu-CNC photonic film and poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer.

(a) Optical image of the C/Glu-CNC photonic film, which easily fractures upon bending. (b) Optical photographs of poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer, showing its ability to recover to its original state after deformation. (c) Optical photographs of C/Glu-CNC photonic film (left) and poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer (right) after immersion in water for 10 min. (d) Stress-strain curves of poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomers prepared with different DMSO swelling times. (e) Stress-strain curves of poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomers prepared with different HDES prepolymer soaking times. (f) Cyclic tensile test of poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer under 50 % strain for ten cycles. (g) Digital photographs of poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomers changing color at different strains. (h) Reflection spectra of poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer under different strains. (i) CIE 1931 color space chromaticity diagram.

Sensing performance of poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer. (a) Signal changes of poly(HDES)/C-CNC sensor under different loads. (b) Change in relative resistance of poly(HDES)/C-CNC sensor attached to a volunteer's wrist when bent. (c) Change in relative resistance of poly(HDES)/C-CNC sensor attached to a finger when bent at different angles. (d) Schematic illustration of information transmission underwater using Morse code. (e–i) Underwater Morse code transmission using poly(HDES)/C-CNC sensor for (e) “HELP”, (f) “SOS”, (g) “YES”, (h) “NO”, and (i) “HELLO”. (j) Resistance response of poly(HDES)/C-CNC sensor during cyclic stretch-recovery at 50 % strain underwater.

head.ce:title: Dynamically mechanochromic, fluorescence-responsive, and underwater sensing cellulose nanocrystal-based conductive elastomers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Co-Innovation Center for Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources, Jiangsu Provincial Key Lab Pulp & Paper Science and Technology, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210037, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Co-Innovation Center for Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources
Jiangsu Provincial Key Lab Pulp & Paper Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Co-Innovation Center for Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources, Jiangsu Provincial Key Lab Pulp & Paper Science and Technology, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210037, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National & Local Joint Engineering Research Center for Mineral Salt Deep Utilization, Huaiyin Institute of Technology, Huaian 223003, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National & Local Joint Engineering Research Center for Mineral Salt Deep Utilization

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National & Local Joint Engineering Research Center for Mineral Salt Deep Utilization, Huaiyin Institute of Technology, Huaian 223003, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Utilizing cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) to mimic biological skin capable of converting external stimuli into optical and electrical signals represents a significant advancement in the development of advanced photonic materials. However, traditional CNC photonic materials typically exhibit static and singular optical properties, with their structural color and mechanical performance being susceptible to water molecules, thereby limiting their practical applications. In this study, CNC-based conductive elastomers with dynamic mechanochromism, fluorescence responsiveness, and enhanced water resistance were developed by incorporating carbon quantum dots (C QDs) and hydrophobic deep eutectic solvents (HDES) into CNC photonic films via an in-situ swelling-photopolymerization method. Additionally, the abundant non-covalent and hydrophobic interactions within poly(HDES) endow the resulting poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer with excellent structural stability, ionic conductivity, and self-adhesive properties, allowing for encrypted information transmission in both air and aquatic environments. The design and application of poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomers hold tremendous potential for various applications, including anti-counterfeiting labels, human-machine interactions, and wearable devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the early 1950s, researchers discovered that cellulose could be used to prepare cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) through acid hydrolysis. Subsequent studies have shown that these CNCs can be utilized to fabricate photonic materials [1–5]. Compared to synthetic photonic crystals, CNCs offer unique advantages due to their abundance, low cost, renewability, and ability to self-assemble into photonic structures. These properties make CNCs highly suitable for applications in structural coatings, sensors, information anti-counterfeiting, and smart packaging [6–9]. The chiral structure of CNCs primarily originates from the d-glucose (Glu) units in cellulose, notably reflected in the helical morphology of CNC-based photonic materials. When the wavelength of incident light matches the helical pitch of the chiral nematic structure of CNCs, the incident light is selectively reflected, resulting in material coloration [10–12]. Excitingly, Vignolini's group has recently achieved large-scale fabrication of CNC photonic materials, paving the way for their industrial application [13].

Due to the inherent rigidity of CNCs and the lack of effective soft energy-dissipation phases, CNC photonic materials are highly brittle and their optical properties are difficult to extend to flexible and mechanically adaptive materials. To address this issue, researchers have attempted to incorporate functional additives (e.g., glycerol, citric acid, poly(ethylene glycol)) into the CNC networks [14–20]. Unfortunately, these composites only exhibit static color variations. Consequently, polymerizable monomers have been introduced into the CNC networks to construct cross-linked matrices to achieve mechanochromic polymer materials [21,22]. Bishnu Acharya et al. immersed CNC/glycerol/poly(vinyl alcohol) composite films in a prepolymer solution containing acrylic monomers to initiate the polymerization reaction, followed by ionic cross-linking using AlCl3, which ultimately resulted in CNC-based photonic hydrogels with reversible color change [22]. Considering that the potential dehydration/ice crystallization issue of hydrogels may affect the stability of structural colors, the development of more versatile and stable CNC photonic materials remains highly desirable.

Deep eutectic solvents (DESs) composed of hydrogen bond donors and acceptors have attracted considerable attention from researchers in recent years due to their remarkable superiority in the synthesis of high-performance polymers [23–28]. Due to the lack of intuitive visualization, DES-based elastomers often require specific equipment to monitor performance changes. This has led to a growing interest in combining these solvents with CNC photonic materials to create multifunctional materials with enhanced visualization. Tian and colleagues have introduced a novel polymerizable DES into the CNC liquid crystal skeleton. Utilizing the inherent elastic, ionic conductive properties of the DES polymer matrix and the dynamic interfacial hydrogen bonding with the CNC network, the resulting CNC-based elastomers exhibited exceptional stretchability, dynamic mechanochromism, ionic conductivity, self-adhesion and self-healing capabilities [29]. Considering interfacial compatibility, most studies tend to employ hydrophilic components to modulate the chiral nematic structure of CNCs. Nevertheless, the water-sensitive nature of these structures compromises their color stability and mechanical performance in humid or underwater conditions. To further improve the environmental adaptability, some hydrophobic components have also been introduced into the CNC networks to construct elastomers with mechanochromic properties. For example, MacLachlan's group explored the incorporation of hydrophobic acrylate monomers into the CNC network, resulting in the preparation of CNC-based elastomers with reversible color changes [30–32]. However, their electrical insulating properties limit their use in flexible electronics. As a result, there is growing interest in exploring the potential of CNC-based elastomers for advancing flexible electronics. These materials not only hold promise for the visualization of flexible devices, but also offer the unique ability to integrate multiple functionalities such as mechanochromism, ionic conductivity, fluorescence response, and enhanced environmental stability.

In this study, CNC networks with multifunctionality and enhanced environmental adaptability can be engineered to achieve in the following two aspects (Fig. 1(a–b)). Firstly, carbon quantum dots (C QDs) are proposed to incorporate into the CNC networks. This modification allows fluorescence under UV light without adversely affecting the structural color of the material. Secondly, the introduction of a meticulously designed polymerizable hydrophobic DES (HDES) with ionic conductivity into the CNC chiral nematic structure is proposed. Through photopolymerization, this approach aims to create a conductive elastomeric network that exhibits both covalent and multiple non-covalent cross-linking properties, thereby endowing the CNC photonic film with dynamic mechanochromic, performance stability and underwater sensing capabilities. The resulting poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomers based on the methodology have promising applications in multi-module sensors, high-performance anti-counterfeiting labels and smart materials for flexible devices.

CNC suspension was purchased from RuiNiu-RN (4 wt%, pH = 5.5). These rod-like CNC particles, derived from the sulfuric acid hydrolysis of wood pulp, exhibited dimensions of 165 ± 14 nm in length and 10 ± 5 nm in width, as supplied by the manufacturer. The sulfuric acid treatment led to the integration of negative sulfate half-ester groups (OSO3 −) on the CNC surface. The Zeta potential was −55.7 mV, ensuring the CNC suspension's stability during storage. Green carbon quantum dots (C QDs, 2–5 nm) were obtained from Pioneer Nano. d-glucose (Glu, AR), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, AR), ethylene glycol phenyl ether acrylate (EGPEA, 90 %), isobornyl acrylate (IBA, 98 %), triethylbenzylammonium chloride (TEBAC, 98 %), thymol (Thy, AR, 98 %), and diphenyl(2,4,6-trimethylbenzoyl)phosphine oxide (TPO, 97 %) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd., and were used as received.

C QDs and Glu (50 mg) were added to the CNC suspension (5.0 g) and sonicated for 1 min. The resulting C/Glu-CNC suspension was poured into a 5 cm diameter polystyrene petri dish. The uncovered petri dish was then placed at 25 °C and 50 % relative humidity for 48 h to undergo an evaporation-induced self-assembly process, ultimately forming the C/Glu-CNC photonic film.

The hydrophobic deep eutectic solvent (HDES) was prepared by mixing two solid components in a specific molar ratio (Thy:TEBAC = 2:1). Typically, 7.51 g Thy and 5.69 g TEBAC were heated and stirred at 80 °C until a homogeneous, colorless solution was formed. The prepared Thy/TEBAC HDES was then stored in a vacuum desiccator for further use.

The two comonomers (EGPEA: IBA = 4:1) and the photoinitiator TPO (molar ratio of 0.1 % to the comonomers) were added to the prepared Thy/TEBAC HDES and stirred evenly. The resulting HDES prepolymer solution was then stored in a vacuum desiccator for further use.

The typical preparation process was as follows: the as-prepared C/Glu-CNC photonic film was placed flat in a mold and then completely immersed in DMSO. After 10 min, the excess DMSO was removed, and a mixture of HDES prepolymer/DMSO (IBA: DMSO = 1:2, mole ratio) was added and allowed to stand in a vacuum desiccator. After 24 h, the liquid mixture was removed with a syringe, and the swollen film was exposed to UV irradiation for 5 min (dominant wavelength of 365 nm, RW-UVA-Φ200U, Shenzhen Runwing Company, China). The light intensity was 20 mW·cm−2 measured using a UV radiometer (type UV-A, Photoelectric Instrument Factory, Beijing Normal University).

The zeta potential was determined by dynamic light scattering measurement (DLS, Malvern ZS Zetasizer, Worcestershire, UK). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained with a Hitachi Regulus 8100, equipped with an Oxford Instruments energy dispersive spectrometer to obtain distribution maps of N and Cl elements on the fracture surface of the films. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded using a Bruker VERTEX 80 V spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a Shimadzu AXIS UltraDLD spectrometer. Reflectance spectra were measured using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Cary60, Agilent, USA) over the wavelength range of 200 to 800 nm at a scanning rate of 600 nm/min.

1H NMR spectra (400 MHz) were recorded using a Bruker AVANCE III HD 400 spectrometer, with DMSO-d 6 as the external reference. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted using an XRD Ultima IV instrument with an X-ray generator power of 3 kW. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed using a NETZSCH instrument, measuring in the temperature range of 30 to 800 °C at a heating rate of 20 °C/min under a nitrogen atmosphere. All characterization tests were conducted under ambient conditions of room temperature (25 °C) and relative humidity (40–60 %).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare the poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer, Glu and C QDs were firstly added to a 4 wt% CNC suspension (Fig. 2(a–b)). Glu served as an additive to effectively suppress the coffee-ring effect and facilitate the formation of a uniform blue color [31]. Additionally, C QDs, as a novel nano-material with unique fluorescent properties, participated in the evaporation-induced self-assembly process [33,34]. As a result, C QDs were uniformly loaded into the CNC network, yielding a composite film material that combined both fluorescence and structural color. As the amount of C QDs increased, the structural color of the CNC photonic film transitioned from blue to light green (Fig. S1), attributed to the increased helical pitch of the CNC film upon the addition of C QDs. Subsequently, DMSO was used to remove Glu from the C/Glu-CNC composite film, providing space for the subsequent infiltration of the HDES prepolymer solution. Due to the integration of polymerizable monomers and conductive ions in the HDES, a wide range of functionalities can be imparted to the synthesized material [28,35]. During this process, the composite film changed color from blue to red. Finally, UV-induced polymerization was performed to obtain the poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer.

Fig. 2(c–e) displayed SEM images of the cross-sectional morphology of the C/Glu-CNC photonic film and the poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer, confirming changes in the helical structure. The C/Glu-CNC photonic film had a small average pitch of approximately 84 nm (Fig. 2(c)). In contrast, the average pitch of the poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer increased to approximately 622 ± 57 nm (Fig. 2(d–e)). Fig. 2(f–g) and Fig. S2 showed the distribution of N and Cl elements on the surface and cross-section of the poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer, indicating a uniform distribution of poly(HDES) throughout the material. XRD patterns demonstrated that both C/Glu-CNC photonic film and poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer contained cellulose I type crystals, with diffraction peaks at 14.9°, 16.5°, 22.8°, and 34.1°, indicating that the crystalline structure of CNC was preserved during the synthesis process (Fig. 2(h)). Fig. 2(i) showed the FT-IR spectra of C/Glu-CNC, HDES, and poly(HDES)/C-CNC, where the presence of CC peaks at 1550 cm−1 and 1650 cm−1 attributed to HDES monomers were significantly reduced in the poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer, indicating the formation of a covalently cross-linked polymer network [35]. Fig. 2(j–k) presented XPS analysis of the C/Glu-CNC photonic film and the poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer. The high-resolution C 1s spectra of the poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer showed new CC, CN, and OCO characteristic peaks at 284.5 eV, 286 eV, and 289 eV, respectively, compared to the C/Glu-CNC photonic film. These results further confirmed the successful incorporation of poly(HDES) into the chiral nematic structure of CNC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced water resistance and mechanochromic response
ce:section.ce:para: Elastic materials prepared using HDES typically exhibit a range of multifunctional properties, including high transparency, stretchability, ionic conductivity, self-adhesion and self-healing. Remarkably, these materials maintain exceptional performance stability even in underwater environments [26,27,36]. Consequently, the integration of poly(HDES) into the CNC network can significantly improve the water resistance of the material. As shown in Fig. 3(a), the C/Glu-CNC photonic film was prone to fracture upon deformation due to the lack of an effective soft dissipation phase. However, the poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer exhibited significant mechanical flexibility due to the covalent and non-covalent cross-linking interactions within the network, allowing it to return to its initial state after deformation (Fig. 3(b)).

Fig. 3(c) demonstrated that when both the C/Glu-CNC photonic film and the prepared poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer were immersed in water for 10 min, the C/Glu-CNC photonic film quickly absorbed water and swelled, leading to a rapid color degradation until becoming colorless, eventually fracturing. However, the structure and color of the poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer remained almost unchanged even after 90 days, indicating that the introduction of poly(HDES) not only improved the water resistance of the elastomer but also helped to maintain its structural color (Figs. S3–S5). Notably, poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer can still emit fluorescence under UV light even underwater (Fig. S6).

The effect of DMSO and HDES prepolymer solution swelling time was investigated in detail during the experiments. As shown in Fig. 3(d), different swelling times in DMSO had little impact on the mechanical properties of the final synthesized elastomer. The DSC curves revealed that the glass transition temperatures of these elastomers exhibited only minor differences (Fig. S7). Especially, the elastomer prepared after swelling for 10 min achieved a strain of approximately 300 % (Fig. 3(d)). Therefore, the optimal DMSO swelling time was chosen to be 10 min. Subsequently, the impact of HDES prepolymer solution soaking time was explored. After swelling the C/Glu-CNC photonic film in DMSO for 10 min, it was then soaked in HDES prepolymer solution for 12 h, 24 h, and 48 h, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3(e), a short soaking time (12 h) resulted in an insufficient amount of HDES prepolymer within the CNC network, leading to unsatisfactory mechanical properties of the synthesized material. When the soaking time was set to 24 h, the elastomer exhibited enhanced tensile deformation and mechanical strength. With further extension of the soaking time, the excess presence of soft poly(HDES) led to increased tensile deformation and a reduction in mechanical strength. Therefore, considering both mechanical strength and tensile deformation, the HDES prepolymer soaking time was optimized to 24 h.

The cyclic tensile performance of the poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomers was further tested. It was observed that after ten continuous tensile-recovery cycles at 50 % strain (Fig. 3(f)), the curve exhibited pronounced hysteresis loops, indicating significant energy dissipation. This was likely due to the partial disruption and insufficient recovery of numerous non-covalent bonds within the network [37]. Dynamic frequency sweep experiments demonstrated that in the lower testing frequency range (<10 Hz), the storage modulus (G′) of the C/Glu-CNC photonic film was significantly higher than its loss modulus (G″), indicating its rigid structure. As the frequency increased, both G′ and G″ oscillated, suggesting instability within the internal network (Fig. S8). In contrast, G′ and G″ of the poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer remained almost constant throughout the scanning range, indicating a stable gel state. Moreover, with increasing temperature, the G′ and G″ of C/Glu-CNC photonic film remained nearly stable, whereas the G′ and G″ of poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer decreased significantly, demonstrating the effective incorporation of soft poly(HDES) into the CNC network (Fig. S9).

Furthermore, the poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer exhibited good adhesion to various inorganic (Fe, glass, ceramics) and organic (wood, rubber, and polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE)) materials both in air, underwater and at low temperature (Figs. S10–S11). This excellent adhesion performance was attributed to the abundant non-covalent interactions within the poly(HDES)/C-CNC network, such as π-π, cation-π, ion-dipole, and hydrogen bonding interactions. When adhering to different substrates, it can exclude interfacial water through hydrophobic interactions, promoting tight contact between poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer and the substrate, thereby achieving efficient adhesion.

The prepared poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer exhibited significant reversible mechanochromic properties (from red to blue) during stretching-recovery process (Fig. 3(g)). Fig. 3(h) showed the reflection spectra of poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer under different tensile strain. As the strain increased from 0 % to 100 %, the reflection wavelength varied from 662 nm to 450 nm. The dynamic changes in structural color were quantitatively characterized using the CIE 1931 color space chromaticity diagram, which showed clear shifts with increasing strain (Fig. 3(i)).

When an external electric field was applied, the ionic migration of TEBAC within the poly(HDES)/C-CNC network allowed it to exhibit ionic conductivity (Fig. S12). When external pressure or deformation was applied, the movement paths of TEBAC conductive ions changed, enabling the poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer to function as a sensor. Meanwhile, the electrical signals can be collected in real-time, allowing effective monitoring and analysis.

As shown in Fig. 4(a), when different loads (20 g, 50 g, 100 g) were applied, the poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer exhibited rapidly and sensitively electrical signal responses. As pressure increased, the ion transport channels were compressed, resulting in a rapid increase in signal strength; when the external pressure was removed, the electrical signal quickly recovered. Due to the good underwater adhesion properties of the poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer, it can be effectively used as a strain sensor firmly attached to a volunteer's body to monitor human activity. Fig. 4(b) showed the changes in electrical signals when the sensor was attached to a volunteer's wrist and subjected to repeated bending underwater. The results showed that the electrical signals remained almost identical during cyclic bending, demonstrating exceptional repeatability. Furthermore, when the sensor was attached to the volunteer's finger underwater, it displayed varying electrical signal intensities at different bending angles, showing its good signal detection capability (Fig. 4(c)). Remarkably, even after poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomers were immersed underwater, its mechanical properties and ionic conductivity changed only slightly, exhibiting overall favourable hydrophobicity and stable ionic conductivity (Figs. S13–S14).

Morse code, consisting of ‘dots’ and ‘dashes’ representing different Latin letters, is widely used in telecommunications to convey precise information (Fig. 4(d)). As a proof of concept, the poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer was employed for encrypted underwater information transmission. Changes in the electrical signal generated by short finger bends were interpreted as ‘dots’, while longer bends represented ‘dashes’. As shown in Fig. 4(e–i), the underwater electrical signal variations successfully recorded the words ‘HELP’, ‘SOS’, ‘YES’, ‘NO’, and ‘HELLO’. Due to its good underwater adhesion and conductivity, the poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer can adhere firmly to human skin for extended periods. As shown in Fig. 4(j), when the film was operated continuously underwater for 1200 s, there was no significant change in the electrical signal, indicating excellent electrical performance. Additionally, as shown in Fig. S15, when two separate pieces of poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer were reconnected, the LED bulb lighted up quickly. This indicated that the soft poly(HDES) polymer facilitated the rapid restoration of the conductive path.

In conclusion, a multifunctional poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer was developed by incorporating C QDs and HDES into a CNC network with a chiral nematic structure. Upon deformation, the poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer underwent a reversible color change, while C QDs imparted fluorescence-responsive properties. The hydrophobic nature of poly(HDES) and dynamic intermolecular interactions among the components endowed the poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomer with enhanced water resistance, allowing it to retain its structural color and mechanical integrity underwater. Additionally, poly(HDES)/C-CNC elastomers exhibited good self-adhesion in both air and aquatic environments. Notably, the elastomers also demonstrated intrinsically ionic conductivity, facilitating underwater sensing and encrypted information transfer. This innovative design holds significant potential for applications in human-machine interfaces, smart sensors, and anti-counterfeiting technologies.

Hongtian Zhang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Yunfeng Cao: Methodology, Conceptualization. Yongke Hu: Validation, Investigation. Zhulan Liu: Conceptualization. Ren'ai Li: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by Opening Funding of National & Local Joint Engineering Research Centre for Mineral Salt Deep Utilization (contract No: SF202203), the Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (22KJB430029) and the Opening Project of Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Chemical Materials and Devices, Ministry of Education, Jianghan University (JDGD202309).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2025.139681.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Centrifuge fractionation during purification of cellulose nanocrystals after acid hydrolysis and consequences on their chiral self-assembly

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.J. Svagan, F. Vilaplana, T. Pettersson, P.R. Anusuyadevi, G. Henriksson, M. Hedenqvist, Centrifuge fractionation during purification of cellulose nanocrystals after acid hydrolysis and consequences on their chiral self-assembly, Carbohydr. Polym. 328 (2024) 121723.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Materials with tunable optical properties for wearable epidermal sensing in health monitoring

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Han, T. Wang, G. Lium, H. Liu, X. Xie, Z. Wei, J. Li, C. Jiang, Y. He, F. Xu, Materials with Tunable Optical Properties for Wearable Epidermal Sensing in Health Monitoring, Adv. Mater. 34(26) (2022) 2109055.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in vacuum assisted self-assembly of cellulose nanocrystals
Curr. Opin. Solid State Mater. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Wang, N. Li, L. Zong, J. Zhang, Recent advances in vacuum assisted self-assembly of cellulose nanocrystals, Curr. Opin. Solid State Mater. Sci. 23(3) (2019) 142-148.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Free-standing mesoporous silica films with tunable chiral nematic structures

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.E. Shopsowitz, H. Qi, W.Y. Hamad, M.J. Maclachlan, Free-standing mesoporous silica films with tunable chiral nematic structures, Nature 468(7322) (2010) 422-425.
